concept.title: Taxonomy and Controlled Vocabulary for AI Classification
concept.shortdesc: How a formal DITA subject scheme taxonomy provides the controlled vocabulary that AI systems need to classify, filter, and route content accurately.

section.title: Why does AI need a controlled vocabulary?
section.p: Without a taxonomy, authors use inconsistent terms for the same concepts. One author writes audience="developers", another writes audience="dev", a third writes audience="engineers". The AI sees these as three different audiences and cannot match them in retrieval.
A controlled vocabulary defines the exact allowed values for each classification facet. A DITA subject scheme map is the standard mechanism for defining and enforcing this vocabulary.

section.title: How to design a content taxonomy for AI
section.p: A production taxonomy needs five classification facets at minimum:

row.entry: Who the content is written for
Filter results by user role; personalize recommendations
Match content level to user expertise; suggest simpler alternatives
dita-fundamentals, authoring, publishing, customization, xslt, devops
Domain-scoped retrieval; only search relevant subject areas
What the content does for the reader
explain, how-to, reference, troubleshoot, best-practice, tutorial
Intent routing; match user question type to content type
Link content to service offerings; enable lead generation

section.title: How to implement a subject scheme in DITA
section.p: The subject scheme is a specialized DITA map that defines the taxonomy:
section.codeblock:
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE subjectScheme PUBLIC "-//OASIS//DTD DITA Subject Scheme Map//EN" "subjectScheme.dtd"> <subjectScheme id="extense-taxonomy"> <title>Extense Content Taxonomy</title>
<!-- AUDIENCE TYPES --> <subjectdef keys="audience-type"> <subjectdef keys="aud-technical-writer"> <topicmeta><navtitle>Technical Writer</navtitle></topicmeta> </subjectdef> <subjectdef keys="aud-developer"> <topicmeta><navtitle>Developer / Engineer</navtitle></topicmeta> </subjectdef> <subjectdef keys="aud-build-engineer"> <topicmeta><navtitle>Build / DevOps Engineer</navtitle></topicmeta> </subjectdef> <subjectdef keys="aud-manager"> <topicmeta><navtitle>Documentation Manager</navtitle></topicmeta> </subjectdef> <subjectdef keys="aud-all"> <topicmeta><navtitle>All Audiences</navtitle></topicmeta> </subjectdef> </subjectdef>
<!-- DIFFICULTY LEVELS --> <subjectdef keys="difficulty-level"> <subjectdef keys="beginner"/> <subjectdef keys="intermediate"/> <subjectdef keys="advanced"/> <subjectdef keys="expert"/> </subjectdef>
<!-- CONTENT DOMAINS --> <subjectdef keys="content-domain"> <subjectdef keys="dom-dita-fundamentals"/> <subjectdef keys="dom-authoring"/> <subjectdef keys="dom-content-reuse"/> <subjectdef keys="dom-publishing"/> <subjectdef keys="dom-customization"/> <subjectdef keys="dom-xslt"/> <subjectdef keys="dom-devops"/> <subjectdef keys="dom-architecture"/> </subjectdef>
<!-- CONTENT INTENT --> <subjectdef keys="content-intent"> <subjectdef keys="explain"/> <subjectdef keys="how-to"/> <subjectdef keys="reference"/> <subjectdef keys="troubleshoot"/> <subjectdef keys="best-practice"/> <subjectdef keys="tutorial"/> <subjectdef keys="overview"/> </subjectdef> </subjectScheme>
section.p: This taxonomy is machine-readable, version-controlled, and can be shared across projects. The chatbot configuration references it for metadata validation.

section.title: How does the glossary support AI term resolution?
section.p: A DITA glossary serves as the chatbot's term resolution engine. Rich glossary entries enable the AI to:

ul.li: Resolve synonyms: User asks about "conditional processing" — the glossary maps this to "DITAVAL filtering"
Expand abbreviations: User references "OT" — the glossary knows this means "DITA Open Toolkit"
Provide definitions: User asks "What does conref mean?" — the glossary provides the canonical definition
Scope applicability: The glossary notes that a feature applies to "DITA-OT 4.x only"

section.p: Example of an AI-optimized glossary entry:
section.codeblock: <glossentry id="gl-conref"> <glossterm>conref</glossterm> <glossdef>A DITA content reference mechanism that pulls content from one topic into another at build time, enabling single-source reuse.</glossdef> <glossBody> <glossSurfaceForm>content reference (conref)</glossSurfaceForm> <glossAlt> <glossAbbreviation>conref</glossAbbreviation> </glossAlt> <glossUsage>Use "conref" when referring to the pull mechanism. Use "content reference" in formal documentation.</glossUsage> <glossScopeNote>Available in all DITA versions. In DITA 1.3+, conkeyref provides indirect conref via keys.</glossScopeNote> </glossBody> </glossentry>

section.title: What's next?
section.p: You now have an information architecture and taxonomy that can organize content at scale. In Part 5: Making Content Intelligent, you will put this architecture to work — enriching topics with AI-ready metadata, migrating content from unstructured XML, and preparing your structured content for chatbot ingestion and intelligent delivery.
